methodsAttempted.query: site:birmingham.ac.uk "University of Birmingham" "generative AI" students assessment policy
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found official student guidance, the main teaching-learning-assessment framework, assessment principles, handbook/Canvas statements, and PGR thesis guidance.
Firecrawl search found official acknowledgement/citation guidance and rejected research publication results that were not policy evidence.

methodsAttempted.query: site:birmingham.ac.uk "AI" "University of Birmingham" data protection research guidance
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found the official research AI guidance DOCX and researcher tool-selection/licensing guidance; news and research-showcase pages were rejected.
robots.txt check confirmed the selected www.birmingham.ac.uk paths were outside listed Disallow paths and advertised the public sitemap index.
robots.txt check showed the selected intranet student/library paths were outside listed Disallow paths.

artifacts.summary: Official University of Birmingham sources were found on www.birmingham.ac.uk and intranet.birmingham.ac.uk, plus one official DOCX under www.birmingham.ac.uk/Documents/research. Firecrawl search and scrape were sufficient; Playwright was not needed. Research-publication and news results were rejected because they were not current institutional AI policy or operational guidance.

targets.sourceTitle: Generative Artificial Intelligence and its role within teaching, learning and assessment
Principles on the Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools to Support Grading and the Provision of Student Feedback
Student and PGR guidance on using GenAI tools ethically for work
Acknowledging and Citing the Use of Generative AI by Students
Programme handbook and Canvas statements relating to the use of Generative AI tools
Student-facing Module Statement on AI Marking and Feedback
Guidance for Academic Schools Relating to PGT Dissertations and the use of Generative Artificial Intelligence Tools
Guidance for the use of AI in research
Responsible AI tool selection for researchers

stopConditions: Stop after official University of Birmingham teaching, student, research, and library/tool-selection guidance sources have enough direct evidence for staged candidate claims.
Do not crawl login-only SharePoint, Canvas, or bham internal pages that require authenticated staff or student sessions.
Reject research publications, news stories, and showcase pages that do not state current University policy or operational guidance.

artifacts.claimText: University of Birmingham maintains a generative AI framework for teaching, learning, assessment, and support.

citation.citationTitle: Generative Artificial Intelligence and its role within teaching, learning and assessment

artifacts.claimText: For assessments and assignments, students should assume generative AI use is not permitted unless the assessment or assignment explicitly states otherwise.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: The student guidance allows use of generative AI tools as study aids for personal learning and research, while distinguishing that from submitting AI-generated output as the student's own assessment work.

artifacts.claimText: Academic staff are expected to state whether and how generative AI tools are permitted in assessments or assignments, including in course outlines, briefs, Canvas pages, and handbooks.

artifacts.claimText: The University states that generative AI detection tools are not currently allowable and that student work should not be uploaded to generative AI detection software.

artifacts.claimText: University-wide AI marking principles allow academic staff to use AI systems to support assessment, grading, moderation and feedback after appropriate approval, while academic staff remain responsible for academic judgements and feedback.

artifacts.claimText: For AI-supported marking and feedback, the University says all decisions, outcomes, and feedback must be reviewed by academic staff before release to students, and generative AI tools alone cannot allocate marks and student grades.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Generative Artificial Intelligence and its role within teaching, learning and assessment
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Tools designed to detect the use of generative AI are currently known to produce both false positives and false negatives. At present, the use of any such tools within the University is not allowable and no student work should be uploaded to generative AI detection software.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Principles on the Use of Artificial Intelligence Tools to Support Grading and the Provision of Student Feedback
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: From the 1 September 2024, and upon the appropriate approval being first received, academic staff can utilise AI systems to support the assessment, grading and moderation of student work along with the provision of individualised student feedback. Where such tools are used, academic staff remain responsible for the academic judgements made on submitted student work and for any feedback they provide for learners.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: All decisions, outcomes and feedback must be reviewed first by an academic member of staff before they are released to students. The use of generative AI tools on their own to allocate marks and student grades is not allowed.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance for Academic Schools Relating to PGT Dissertations and the use of Generative Artificial Intelligence Tools
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Students should not submit, within any part of their PGT dissertation, material or content that has been generated by AI tools unless their use has been specifically permitted by the School. Where the use of generative AI tools is permitted, the University's Framework for the Introduction and Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence within Teaching, Learning and Assessment must be followed and students required to appropriately reference its use.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance for the use of AI in research
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: This guidance applies to all researchers at the University of Birmingham who engage with Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the context of research, whether by using existing tools, developing new models, or deploying AI systems in real-world environments. A human researcher must be accountable for every substantive claim, interpretation, and output.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Guidance for the use of AI in research - Scope and Human Oversight
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance for the use of AI in research
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Personal, confidential, or sensitive data must not be entered into AI tools without: clear justification (including consideration of locally-hosted versus cloud-based tools), data minimisation, Data Protection Impact Assessments (where applicable).
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Guidance for the use of AI in research - Data Protection And Information Security

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidance for the use of AI in research

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI tools licensing review guidance
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Before registering for a new tool, it is crucial to review the Terms and Conditions (licence), which form the legal agreement between you and the supplier. If issues arise, such as non-compliance with accessibility or data protection standards, indemnification requirements, or copyright concerns, seek further guidance from your local licensing or IT procurement teams before registering for the service.

artifacts.title: University of Birmingham staged AI policy crawl
artifacts.summary: Staged machine-reviewed candidates for University of Birmingham based on official university sources only. The run found official guidance covering a university-level generative AI framework, student assessment rules, staff communication requirements, AI marking and feedback principles, PGT dissertation guidance, research AI governance, data protection, endorsed tools, and licensing review. Research-publication and news results were rejected as non-policy evidence.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
Coverage is limited to official University of Birmingham sources that were publicly fetchable without login or access-control bypass during this run.
SharePoint and Canvas resources linked from public pages were not crawled because they appear to require authenticated University access.
The staged claims are agent-reviewed candidates only and are not published as canonical public policy claims.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
